name: Jerusalem: City of Peace, City of Conflict
longDescription:
Jerusalem stands unique among the world's cities as the spiritual center for three major
                monotheistic religions: Judaism, Christianity, and Islam. No other place on Earth carries such
                concentrated religious significance across diverse faith traditions. Located in the Judaean
                Mountains between the Mediterranean Sea and the Dead Sea, this ancient city has been destroyed
                and rebuilt multiple times, conquered by empires from Babylonia to Rome to Britain, yet has
                remained the focal point of faith, longing, and pilgrimage for billions of believers throughout
                history. For Jews, Jerusalem represents the eternal capital, the location of the First and Second Temples,
                the city of King David and King Solomon, and the site toward which prayers have been directed
                for nearly three millennia. The Western Wall, the last remnant of the Second Temple complex,
                serves as the holiest site in Judaism where prayers can be offered. The entire city resonates
                with Jewish memory, from the ancient City of David to modern neighborhoods bearing biblical
                names.
searchTerms: jerusalem: city of peace, city of conflict
sacred crossroads of judaism, christianity, and islam
description:
Explore temples, mountains, groves, and holy sites that have served as gateways between the human and divine realms
                across cultures and millennia. From ancient stone circles to living pilgrimage destinations, discover the places
                where heaven touches earth.
significance:
Jerusalem stands unique among the world's cities as the spiritual center for three major
                monotheistic religions: Judaism, Christianity, and Islam. No other place on Earth carries such
                concentrated religious significance across diverse faith traditions.

geography: Located in the West Bank, Eastern Mediterranean, approximately 750m elevation. Old City contains Western Wall, Church of the Holy Sepulchre, and Dome of the Rock. Built on hills with ancient water systems. Strategic position on trade routes
relatedEvents: Islamic conquest and establishment of sanctuaries

keyMyths.title: The Binding of Isaac (Akedah)
keyMyths.description: Abraham's willingness to sacrifice his son Isaac at God's command on Mount Moriah (believed by some to be the Temple Mount) is a pivotal story in Judaism, Christianity, and Islam, demonstrating faith and obedience. God ultimately provides a ram as a substitute sacrifice, highlighting divine mercy and the sanctity of human life.

keyMyths.title: Jacob's Dream
keyMyths.description: While fleeing from his brother Esau, Jacob dreams of a ladder extending from earth to heaven, with angels ascending and descending. God appears to him and reaffirms the covenant made with Abraham, promising Jacob's descendants the land of Canaan, solidifying the land's divine importance.

keyMyths.title: The Temple of Solomon
keyMyths.description: King Solomon builds the First Temple in Jerusalem, a magnificent structure intended as the permanent dwelling place of God and the Ark of the Covenant. It becomes the center of Jewish worship and national identity, symbolizing God's presence among his people until its destruction by the Babylonians.

keyMyths.title: Jesus' Ministry, Crucifixion, and Resurrection
keyMyths.description: Jerusalem is central to the Christian narrative, as the site of Jesus' ministry, including his teachings, miracles, and entry into the city. He is crucified, dies, and is resurrected within Jerusalem, establishing it as the holiest city in Christianity.
keyMyths.source: Gospels of Matthew, Mark, Luke, and John (New Testament)

keyMyths.title: The Night Journey (Isra and Mi'raj)
keyMyths.description: In Islamic tradition, the Prophet Muhammad miraculously travels from Mecca to Jerusalem (Isra) in a single night and ascends to heaven (Mi'raj) from the Temple Mount. During his ascent, Muhammad meets with previous prophets and receives instructions from God, solidifying Jerusalem's status as a holy city in Islam.
keyMyths.source: Quran (Surah 17:1); Hadith literature (accounts of Muhammad's life and teachings)

sources.name: The Hebrew Bible/Old Testament
sources.description: A collection of ancient religious texts of Judaism and Christianity, containing narratives, laws, poetry, and prophecies.
sources.title: The Hebrew Bible/Old Testament

sources.name: The New Testament
sources.description: The second part of the Christian biblical canon, focusing on the life, teachings, death, and resurrection of Jesus Christ.
sources.title: The New Testament

sources.name: The Quran
sources.description: The central religious text of Islam, believed by Muslims to be the word of God (Allah) revealed to the Prophet Muhammad.
sources.title: The Quran

sources.name: Karen Armstrong, *Jerusalem: One City, Three Faiths*
sources.description: A comprehensive history of Jerusalem, exploring its significance in Judaism, Christianity, and Islam and the conflicts surrounding it.
sources.title: Karen Armstrong, *Jerusalem: One City, Three Faiths*

sources.name: Simon Sebag Montefiore, *Jerusalem: The Biography*
sources.description: A biographical history of Jerusalem, focusing on the individuals and events that have shaped its identity and destiny.
sources.title: Simon Sebag Montefiore, *Jerusalem: The Biography*
